Section 9.10c (page 361) Explain why a lake on a bed of limestone is naturally buffered against the effects of acid rain: Acid Rain and a Naturally Buffered Lake Unpolluted rainwater is not & neutral solution with a pH of 7; rather; because it contains dissolved carbon dioxide, it is slightly acidic with 3 pH of about 5.6. COzlg) 2 HzO() HzOt(aq) HCOz-(aq) carbon dioxide from the air low concentration of Hzot gives rainwater a pH < 7.
Rainwater that contains dissolved HzSO4 (or HNOg from burning fossil fuels has a pH lower than 5.6. In some parts of the United States, rainwater often has a pH range of 4-5, and readings as low as pH = 1.8 have been recorded. When the rain in & region consistently has a lower-than-normal pH, this acid rain can have & devastating effect on plant and animal life.
The pH of some lakes changes drastically aS the result of acid rain, whereas the pH of other lakes does not. In fact, the ability of some lakes to absorb acid rain without much pH change is entirely due to buffers Figure 9.11) Lakes that are surrounded by limestonerich soil are in contact with solid calcium carbonate, CaCOz As a result; the lake contains & natural carbonate/bicarbonate buffer: When acid precipitation falls on the lake, the dissolved carbonate (CO; 2- ) reacts with the acid to form bicarbonate (HCO; )
The buffer reacts with added acid from rain:
CO3 2-(aq)
Hzot(aq)
HCO3 (aq)
HzO()
lake surrounded by limestone contains a natural CO3 2-IHCO3 buffer
